---
title: "MERIT-DE: robust, FDR-safe, covariate-aware differential expression"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{MERITde}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, eval=FALSE}
library(MERITde)
# counts: integer gene-by-sample matrix; group: two-level factor
res <- merit_de(counts, group)
head(res[order(res$padj), ])
sum(res$padj < 0.05)                      # significant DEGs at FDR 5%

# adjust for a confounder (batch / age) -- Wilcoxon cannot do this
res_adj <- merit_de(counts, group, covariates = data.frame(batch = batch))
```

## Why MERIT-DE

* **Robust** — a Winsorized transform caps outlier observations, the main driver
  of the exaggerated false positives that DESeq2/edgeR show on large human
  cohorts (Li et al., Genome Biology 2022).
* **Powerful** — empirical-Bayes moderation (limma) borrows information across
  genes, matching limma-voom/Wilcoxon power across sample sizes.
* **FDR-safe** — a guarded empirical-null calibration (Efron 2004) tightens FDR
  control without sacrificing power, and never inflates.
* **Covariate-aware** — adjust for batch/age/sex via the design matrix.
* **Fast** — typically the fastest of the common DE methods.
